label: AI · EN

hero.title:
AI without magic:
from chatbot to dogfood loop
hero.subtitle: (agents, skills, cron, state files, and bugs found while you sleep.)
hero.lead: A scribbled-in field guide to AI, agents, dogfooding, the Claude Code gist pattern, state files, cron, skills, tests, safety rails, and why autonomy works only when it has a fence.
hero.note: ← drag the thumb tabs on the right · flip cards · tick checklists →

welcomeStickies.html:
✎ How to read this:

scroll, flip cards, match terms, tick checklists. the reflection boxes save your notes, so argue with the page.

✎ Upgrade note:

this version explains the gist as a setup wizard: it does not magically dogfood your app; it creates the state file, cron prompt, safety rails, and recovery path.

✎ Promise:

no equations. no corporate fog. no "just trust the agent." every autonomous trick gets a brake pedal.

tocTitle: The table of contents, now with fewer robot myths

levels.label: BASIC
levels.intro: — the parts you need before the agent starts clicking things on your behalf.

lessons.title: An LLM is a statistical parrot with a badge

sticky.html: Imagine a parrot that read the whole internet, memorized ways people talk, saw millions of examples, and now answers as if it understood. Sometimes it is right. Sometimes it invents things while wearing a tie.

blocks.html:
An LLM is not a mind trapped inside a computer. It is a model that turns text into numbers, uses those numbers to predict the next piece of text, and repeats that many times. Intelligence appears as a side effect of lots of well-trained prediction.

The fancy term is "language model." The honest term is: autocomplete studied abroad, read Stack Overflow, and came back way too confident.
blocks.placeholder: polaroid: a very serious parrot in a lab coat, sitting on a mountain of books, looking way too confident

blocks.label: WHAT GOES IN, WHAT COMES OUT
blocks.caption: the trick is not one whole answer at once; it is a chain of tiny bets.

blocks.html: A model can sound brilliant and still be wrong. Fluency is not truth.
blocks.by: the margin note you should tattoo on every AI demo

bad.label: CUTE MYTH
bad.html: "AI understands the world like a person, just faster."

good.label: MORE USEFUL
good.html: "AI learned language patterns and uses those patterns to generate answers that can look like reasoning."

blocks.title: Under the hood — parameters, training, and hallucination
blocks.html:
During training, the model sees examples and adjusts billions of parameters: small internal numbers that influence what it predicts. Nobody writes a manual rule for every answer. The system learns statistical regularities.

When it is confidently wrong, we call it a hallucination. It is not a lie in the human sense. It is a plausible prediction that was not anchored to an external sensor, document, test, or tool.

That is why serious AI work keeps asking for an external sensor: a test, a citation, a database query, a human review, a log line. The model is useful. The sensor is how you stop worshiping its confidence.

items.question: Why can a model explain a concept well and miss a simple date?
items.answer: Because plausible language is not the same as verification. Without a source, tool, or reliable memory, it is estimating the answer from patterns.

items.question: What is the first practical question when using an LLM?
items.answer: "Does it only need to write, or does it need to verify something in the world?" If it needs to verify, you want a tool, source, test, or external data.

blocks.prompt: Think of an AI answer that felt convincing. What external sensor would have helped verify it?
blocks.hint: e.g. official link, automated test, logs, database, responsible human...

lessons.title: Context is the model's backpack

sticky.html: Imagine a hiking trail. You can only carry what fits in your backpack. Pack too much? The flashlight falls out. Later you ask, "where is the flashlight?" and the backpack says, "what flashlight?"

blocks.html:
The model does not remember the human way. The application sends a context window with instructions, conversation, files, and tool results. Anything that does not fit in that backpack becomes archaeological history.

That is why long chats get weird: the assistant did not "get dumb." It is trying to run a marathon while carrying receipts, screenshots, your anxiety, and three broken commits.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: an overstuffed backpack bursting with papers, tokens falling out of the zipper, a flashlight on the ground

blocks.label: CHAT MEMORY VS REAL STATE
blocks.caption: context is great for thinking right now; persistent state is better for continuing tomorrow.

messages.text: Remember this: my test user is luna@example.dev.
Got it. I can use that while it remains in the current context.
...50 messages later... who is the test user?
I may not have that earlier detail anymore. The backpack got too full.

bad.label: COMMON CONFUSION
bad.html: "The agent already knows what it did yesterday because the conversation was open."

good.label: GROUNDED VIEW
good.html: "If the work needs to continue after a fire, write it to disk. A conversation is not a database."

blocks.title: Under the hood — tokens are not perfect words
blocks.html:
Text becomes tokens: pieces of words, symbols, spaces, and fragments. Context limits are counted in those pieces, not in human pages. Big files, logs, and tool-call history can fill the window quickly.

In the dogfood loop, that becomes a real problem: every cron fire adds more conversation. The state file reduces dependence on session memory, but it does not stop linear context growth.

items.question: Why does a state file help more than "continue where you left off"?
items.answer: Because it turns progress into an artifact. The model can reread a short, current file instead of depending on a giant, fragile conversation.

items.question: What would you save in the state file of a bug-hunting agent?
items.answer: Scenarios, run counts, findings, created branches, opened issues, stop time, loop status, and a log line for each fire.

blocks.prompt: What task of yours would fail if the agent forgot what it had already tried? What minimum information would you save in a state file?
blocks.hint: think about ticket ID, last step executed, decision made, observed result...

lessons.title: An agent is an intern with tools

sticky.html: You do not say: "move the mouse 12 pixels, click, open the terminal, run the test." You say: "figure out why login broke." The intern looks around, grabs tools, and tries not to knock over production.

blocks.html:
A chatbot replies. An agent decides the next step, uses a tool, observes the result, and decides again. The practical magic is the loop: think → act → observe.

The difference between "chatbot" and "agent" is the difference between someone giving you a cake recipe and someone putting flour all over your kitchen. The second option requires trust and a mop.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: an intern wearing an 'agent' badge, carrying a browser, terminal, magnifying glass, and a tiny fire extinguisher

blocks.html: ← tools turn talk into action

blocks.label: THE AGENTIC LOOP
blocks.caption: when the environment answers back, the agent gets feedback. without feedback, it only writes nicely.

blocks.html:
You: Run the signup flow and tell me if anything breaks.
↳ opening browser...
↳ filling signup form...
↳ found error: email confirmation button hidden

blocks.title: Under the hood — ReAct without turning into a lecture
blocks.html:
The classic LLM-agent pattern interleaves reasoning and action: the model chooses an action, receives an observation, updates the plan, and keeps going. In coding-agent practice, those actions are tools: Bash, file reading, editing, browser, GitHub, cron.

The grown-up detail: each tool increases power and risk. Reading a file is cheap. Editing code is more serious. Creating an issue is reversible. Pushing code is another planet.

items.question: When does a chatbot become an agent?
items.answer: When it does not just reply, but chooses and executes actions in an environment: terminal, browser, files, APIs, database.

items.question: What is the safety question before giving a tool?
items.answer: "Is the damage from a wrong action reversible?" The less reversible it is, the more human approval and caps you want.

blocks.prompt: Pick one tool you would give an agent and one you would not. What changes between them?
blocks.hint: e.g. read logs vs delete data; open issue vs deploy; run test vs push.

lessons.title: Dogfooding is eating your own dog food

sticky.html: You manufacture dog food. Before selling it, someone in the house needs to taste it. If even the factory dog makes a face, the customer probably will too.

blocks.html:
In software, dogfooding means using your own product to discover pain before users discover it. In the dogfood loop, the one using the app is an agent with a browser. It becomes a tireless, slightly literal user that clicks around and complains in Markdown.

It is QA with insomnia. Except instead of coffee, it consumes context.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: a robot dog testing a bowl of dog food labeled 'staging app'

blocks.label: WHAT THE DOGFOOD LOOP DOES
blocks.caption: the loop is not just navigation; it is navigation + triage + limited action + recording.

bad.label: PURE MANUAL QA
bad.html: "Open the app when you can, click a few things, and send a screenshot in Slack."

good.label: DOGFOOD LOOP
good.html: "Run a defined scenario, save evidence, classify by criteria, write an issue or minimal fix, and log the run."

blocks.label: GIST TRANSLATION
blocks.html:
A GitHub Gist is just a shareable snippet or note. This particular gist is a paste-and-go setup wizard: you paste it into Claude Code, answer configuration questions, and it writes the artifacts that make the loop possible.

The dogfood loop is the dog. The gist is the person who sets out the bowl, timer, leash, and escape plan.

blocks.title: Under the hood — the gist is the setup, not the miracle
blocks.html:
The gist works as a wizard: it asks configuration questions, discovers the environment, creates the state file, and schedules the prompt. The real loop happens later, on each scheduled fire.

The architecture has three legs: cron as driver, self-contained prompt as program, and state file as durable memory. Remove one, and the robot limps.

items.question: Why does the agent use a browser instead of only reading code?
items.answer: Because many bugs only appear in real behavior: hidden button, confusing flow, tracking error, visual state, slow response, broken screen.

items.question: Why does dogfooding not replace human QA?
items.answer: Because the agent finds signals and executes patterns. Humans still judge product, priority, intention, and impact.

blocks.prompt: Which flow in your app/project would be perfect for a robot dog to test every hour?
blocks.hint: look for a repetitive, important flow with clear evidence of success or failure.

levels.label: INTERMEDIATE
levels.topics: Gist · Prompt as Program · Skills · State · Cron · Wizard
levels.intro: — now we turn concepts into a repeatable dogfood loop.

lessons.title: The gist is a setup wizard, not the dog

sticky.html: Imagine an escape room host who does not solve the puzzle for you. They set the timer, hand out the rules, check the doors, place the clue cards, and point to the emergency exit.

blocks.html:
That is what the gist does. It is the installer for the loop, not the loop itself. The actual dogfooding happens later, each time the scheduled prompt fires.

A bad read of the gist is: “Claude runs for 24 hours because vibes.” A better read is: “a setup prompt generates durable-ish state, a cron prompt, scenario rotation, and guardrails.” Less mystical. More useful.
blocks.placeholder: polaroid: escape-room host with a clipboard, stopwatch, clue cards, and a big red emergency stop button

blocks.label: WHAT THE GIST SETS UP
blocks.caption: the gist is a bootstrapper: it creates the machinery that later runs the loop.

blocks.title: ~/repo — dogfood setup wizard

lines.text: paste wizard.md into Claude Code
detecting project: repo, branch, package manager, scripts
found /dogfood-explore skill and agent-browser
pre-flight: trunk ok, URL 200, output dir gitignored
created dogfood-output/loop-state.md
rendered hourly cron prompt: read state → run scenario → triage → fix or issue → update state
waiting for explicit approval before CronCreate

bad.label: MISREADING THE GIST
bad.html:
“It is a magic prompt that makes Claude autonomous for 24 hours.”

That explanation skips the architecture, which is the only reusable part.

good.label: PRACTICAL READING
good.html: “It is a wizard that builds a bounded agent loop: persistent state, scenario skills, pre-flight checks, a self-contained scheduled prompt, conservative triage, and a recovery protocol.”

blocks.label: WHAT YOU WOULD COPY INTO YOUR OWN DOC
blocks.html: The gist is a paste-and-go setup prompt: paste it into Claude Code, answer the config questions, let it generate the state file and scheduled prompt, review, then arm the cron. Do not copy the exact gist blindly. Copy its sections: mission, inputs, discovery checks, state file schema, cron prompt, decision tree, fix policy, kill criteria, and how to stop/recover.

blocks.title: Under the hood — why the gist is written like infrastructure, not a tutorial
blocks.html:
The wizard uses interactive elicitation: ask only the missing configuration, offer detected defaults first, and avoid making the user type facts the filesystem already knows. That is why it checks Git, lockfiles, package scripts, dogfood skills, browser tooling, URL health, dirty worktree state, and GitHub labels.

Then it renders a prompt capsule: one scheduled prompt containing mission, variables, commands, constraints, category criteria, output format, and stop conditions. The scheduled fire should not depend on “what we discussed earlier.” It should be able to wake up cold, read the state file, and continue.

The deeper pattern: a document that writes the operating procedure. The gist is not just instructions for Claude. It is a small infrastructure generator written in natural language.

items.question: Why does the gist ask questions instead of assuming everything?
items.answer: Because autonomy needs configuration: URL, trunk branch, scenarios, time window, commands, output dir, and issue label. Guessing those wrong can waste hours or mutate the wrong place.

items.question: Why does the gist ask before arming cron?
items.answer: Because scheduling creates repeated action. Repeated action deserves an explicit human checkpoint. Paste-and-go should not mean paste-and-hope.

blocks.prompt: Imagine writing your own setup gist. What are the 7 questions it should ask before it is allowed to schedule anything?
blocks.hint: Think URL, branch, scenarios, timebox, test commands, output/state location, labels, auth, permissions, and stop criteria.

lessons.title: A good prompt is an executable recipe

sticky.html: Bad recipe: "make a nice dinner." Good recipe: ingredients, order, timing, when to stop, what to do if something burns, and the exact sentence: "do not invent flambé if the stove is already on fire."

blocks.html:
In the dogfood loop, the prompt is not just a request. It is a program: it contains goal, variables, constraints, category criteria, commands, expected output, and stop condition. Each fire must carry its own brain inside the prompt.

A self-contained prompt is like a packed lunch: if you forgot the fork, complaining to the microwave will not help.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: a cooking recipe covered in XML tags, timer, checklist, and a sign saying 'do not skip steps'

blocks.label: ANATOMY OF A PROMPT CAPSULE
blocks.caption: the prompt becomes an operational contract, not a motivational paragraph.

annotations.text: ↖ rules that do not depend on memory
↑ final format that is easy to verify

blocks.html:
<critical_constraints>
  NEVER push. NEVER commit to trunk.
  Max 3 fixes. Read state first, write last.
</critical_constraints>

<categorization_criteria>
  backend_fix → tracking_fix → ui_issue → needs_product
</categorization_criteria>

<output_format>
  [LOOP] scenario=X fixes=Y issues=Z status=ACTIVE
</output_format>
← tags are road signs, not XML homework

blocks.title: Under the hood — why use XML-ish tags?
blocks.html:
Tags like <critical_constraints> and <categorization_criteria> work like traffic signs inside the prompt. They separate instruction, context, criteria, and output format.

They do not need to be valid XML. The value is structure: the model is less likely to treat an example as an order, a rule as a comment, or expected output as a cute suggestion.

Think of the final prompt as a compiled artifact. The wizard collects variables, substitutes them into the template, checks that no placeholders remain, and only then offers to schedule it.

items.question: Why does the cron prompt need to be self-contained?
items.answer: Because each scheduled fire must work like a capsule. It cannot depend on an old conversation, implicit context, or you reminding the agent.

items.question: What does a fragile prompt smell like?
items.answer: It says "as agreed above," "continue from there," or "do it the usual way." That only works until memory betrays you.

blocks.prompt: Take an automation you would ask an agent to run. What three mandatory sections would your prompt-program have?
blocks.hint: e.g. goal, constraints, success criteria, output format, rollback plan.

lessons.title: Skills are recipe cards in the agent's pocket

sticky.html: A chef does not relearn lasagna every day. They have a recipe. A good agent should not rediscover login, checkout, build, and deploy every time it wakes up either.

blocks.html:
A skill packages instructions, scripts, and materials for a recurring task. In the dogfood loop, each scenario can be a skill: "test checkout," "explore onboarding," "run login with browser." The skill turns repeatable knowledge into a named tool.

Without a skill, the agent is a tourist asking where the bathroom is on every corner. With a skill, it at least has a map. It may still open the wrong door.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: recipe cards held together by a ring, each named '/dogfood-checkout' or '/dogfood-login'

blocks.label: SCENARIO SKILL
blocks.html:
NAME →/dogfood-checkout
WHEN →when the loop selects the checkout scenario
HOW →open app, navigate, gather evidence, list findings

annotations.text: ↖ the loop picks one per fire

blocks.html:
dogfood-login
logs in, authenticates, checks errors
dogfood-checkout
cart to fake payment
dogfood-onboarding
first-time experience
dogfood-explore
generic if none exist

good.label: USEFUL SKILL
good.html: "Open the target URL, complete the signup flow, capture a screenshot for each failure, and return findings with repro steps. Do not fix inside the skill."

blocks.title: Under the hood — skill, command, and tool are not the same thing
blocks.html:
A tool is an executable capability: Bash, browser, file reading, GitHub. A skill is a recipe that guides the model to use tools in a specific way. A command is a way to invoke a recipe by name.

In the loop, agent-browser is the hand on the browser; the skill is the tour script; the cron prompt is the manager that chooses which tour to run today.

items.question: Why rotate scenario skills?
items.answer: So you do not spend every fire on the same flow. Rotation gives coverage: login, checkout, onboarding, and other areas all get a turn.

items.question: What happens if no skill exists?
items.answer: The wizard can create a generic explorer, but that is only a starting point. A specific scenario always teaches the agent better.

blocks.title: MATCH THE AGENT WORDS

pairs.right: an executable capability, like Bash, browser, or GitHub
a reusable recipe that tells the agent when and how to use tools
one user flow to dogfood, such as signup or checkout
the tiny routing hint that decides when a skill loads

blocks.prompt: Write the names of three skills your project should have. Would one be generic or specific? Why?

lessons.title: A state file is the agent's save game

sticky.html: In a video game, you beat a level and save. The console can turn off. Your character does not return to the tutorial, because progress became a file.

blocks.html:
The state file is exactly that: truth written to disk. The agent reads it at the beginning and writes it at the end. Processes die; files survive.

Without a state file, your agent is that friend who asks for the same address five times and still shows up at the wrong house.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: a video-game screen saying 'SAVE COMPLETE' with pixel-art tables of scenarios, issues, and branches

blocks.label: READ FIRST, WRITE LAST
blocks.caption: if it fails midway, the next fire still has a map of what happened before.

blocks.html: Processes die. Files survive. That is the whole trick.
blocks.by: state-file gospel, probably written on a whiteboard somewhere

blocks.title: dogfood-output/loop-state.md

lines.text: # Dogfood Loop State
Loop Status: ACTIVE
Hard stop: 2026-05-26 19:57   Cadence: 7 * * * *
| /dogfood-login | runs=1 | active | last auto=1 |
| /dogfood-checkout | runs=0 | active | last — |
Active fix branches: fix/dogfood-login-email
Run log: append one line per fire; never erase history.

bad.label: IMPLICIT MEMORY
bad.html: "The model will remember that it already opened an issue for this."

good.label: EXPLICIT STATE
good.html: "Issue #123 is registered in the state file; if the bug appears again, the agent can see it is not new."

blocks.title: Under the hood — why Markdown?
blocks.html:
Markdown is readable by humans and by LLMs. JSON is great for rigid machines; Markdown is great for a mixture of tables, notes, and logs that you can edit by hand.

The point is not that the format is sacred. The point is having persistent external state. It can be Markdown, JSON, SQLite, or a database. For this loop, Markdown is cheap and enough.

items.question: Why should the state file be written at the end?
items.answer: Because you want to record the result of what actually happened, not the intention. First observe, then save the new state.

items.question: What is the risk of two agents writing the same state file at the same time?
items.answer: A write race: one overwrites the other's progress. Then you need a lock, queue, separate branch, or database with concurrency control.

blocks.prompt: Mentally draw the state file for your automation. Which fields would keep the agent from repeating work?
blocks.hint: think about already-processed IDs, last timestamp, attempts, status, evidence links.

lessons.title: Cron is the alarm clock that pokes the agent

sticky.html: You do not wake up at 3 a.m. to ask if the deploy finished. You set an alarm. Software does the same thing, except with expressions that look like sad Wi‑Fi passwords.

blocks.html:
Cron schedules prompts. In the gist, every hour it fires the dogfood-loop capsule. Time becomes the trigger. The agent does not wait for you to message it; it wakes up, reads the state file, and runs the next scenario.

Cron is old, ugly, and everywhere. Like duct tape in infrastructure.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: a ringing alarm clock showing '7 * * * *', poking a sleepy robot

blocks.label: ANATOMY OF CRON
blocks.html:
7  *  *  *  *
↑
minute
↑
hour
↑
day
↑
month
↑
weekday

translation: every hour, at minute 7.

blocks.title: ~/.claude — scheduled dogfood fire

lines.text: CronCreate cron="7 * * * *" recurring=true
queued low-priority scheduled prompt
[LOOP] scenario=/dogfood-login runs=2 auto_fixable=1 fixes=1 issues=1 loop_status=ACTIVE
next fire will pick the least-run active scenario
(missed fires do not magically catch up; design for that.)

bad.label: LOOP INSIDE THE CHAT
bad.html:
"Keep working forever until you finish."

Sounds simple. Also sounds like an open faucet.

good.label: CRON WITH A TIMEBOX
good.html:
"Wake up every hour, run one scenario, log one line, stop if the hard stop is hit."

Less drama. More control.

blocks.title: Under the hood — session-scoped is not immortal
blocks.html:
In Claude Code, session-scoped scheduled tasks live in the current conversation. They can be restored with --resume or --continue if they have not expired, but closing or replacing the session changes the scheduling story. The durable part of the gist pattern is the state file, not a promise that the in-session scheduler is immortal.

Claude Code cron uses standard five-field cron expressions like 7 * * * *. Scheduled prompts run between turns and may be delayed by scheduler jitter, so a dogfood loop should treat time as “rough cadence,” not as a stopwatch. If exact unattended reliability matters, move the driver to Desktop scheduled tasks, managed routines, GitHub Actions, or OS cron.

items.question: Why run once per hour instead of nonstop?
items.answer: Because intervals reduce consumption, create time to review, limit blast radius, and prevent one error from becoming a machine gun of commits.

items.question: What does "no catch-up" mean in scheduling?
items.answer: If the agent missed three time slots while it was busy, you do not necessarily want three cascading runs. One controlled fire is enough.

blocks.prompt: What cadence would make sense for one of your agents: minute, hour, night, or week? What would happen if it ran too fast?
blocks.hint: think about cost, context, external state, risk, and the value of finding problems early.

lessons.title: The wizard is an airport check-in

sticky.html: Before flying, you go through check-in, documents, bags, gate, and that existential stare at the scale. Nobody wants to discover mid-air that they forgot fuel.

blocks.html:
The gist starts with a wizard: it discovers repo, branch, package manager, scripts, skills, and agent-browser. Then it asks questions with defaults and runs pre-flight checks. Good setup removes dumb questions and finds problems early.

An agent that asks "do you use npm?" while staring at a pnpm-lock.yaml deserves a seat in the back row.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: an airport counter with bags tagged 'repo', 'branch', 'skills', 'agent-browser' and a robot checking passports

blocks.label: FROM PASTE-AND-GO TO ARMED CRON
blocks.caption: the wizard is not decoration; it is the difference between automation and a scheduled accident.

annotations.text: ↖ human choices only where they matter

blocks.html:
Q2 Trunk: main (Detected)
Q3 Scenarios: /dogfood-login, /dogfood-checkout
Q5 Checks: pnpm lint && pnpm test && pnpm build
pre-flight: branch ok · url 200 · label exists · tree clean

blocks.label: WRITE YOUR OWN LOOP SPEC
blocks.html: A good loop document reads like an operations runbook: what it may do, what it must never do, how it proves work, where it writes state, and when it stops. If a sleepy teammate cannot audit it at 2am, it is too clever.

items: name the mission in one sentence
list the scenarios or skills to rotate through
define the state file sections before writing the prompt
write pre-flight checks that fail before the loop is armed
separate reversible actions from destructive actions
write kill criteria and recovery commands

bad.label: LAZY SETUP
bad.html: "Paste this huge prompt and hope you are in the right repo."

good.label: SETUP WITH WIZARD
good.html: "Discover the environment, ask only what is necessary, validate preconditions, show the rendered prompt, and only then arm cron."

blocks.title: Under the hood — parallel discovery
blocks.html:
STEP 1 runs multiple checks in parallel: git root, remote, branch, lockfiles, scripts, existing skills, and agent-browser. That is environment introspection: the agent uses the filesystem as the source, not your patience.

Pre-flight is fail-fast: if the trunk branch does not exist, the URL does not respond, or the tree is dirty, it is better to stop now than wake up to 24 hours of automated garbage.

items.question: Why are detected defaults so important?
items.answer: Because they reduce friction without removing control. The user presses Enter when detection is right and only thinks about special cases.

items.question: What is the goal of a dry-fire?
items.answer: To run one manual fire before recurring automation. It is the dress rehearsal before leaving the robot alone.

blocks.prompt: Which pre-flight check would prevent your automation from doing something dumb?
blocks.hint: e.g. clean git tree, staging environment, correct token, backup, scope limit, forbidden branch.

levels.label: ADVANCED
levels.topics: Decision Trees · TDD · Bounded Autonomy · L4 Gaps
levels.intro: — the guardrails that decide whether autonomy is useful or just expensive chaos.

lessons.title: A decision tree is the club bouncer

sticky.html: At the club door, the bouncer does not debate philosophy. There is a list, criteria, and order. Right wristband gets in. Sketchy document stays outside. Simple, boring, necessary.

blocks.html:
The loop classifies each finding with strict criteria: backend_fix, tracking_fix, ui_issue, needs_product. The first matching category wins; when in doubt, it falls to the safe option. Classification decides whether the agent may edit code or only open an issue.

Every dangerous automation needs a bored adult saying "no." Here, that adult is the decision tree.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: club bouncer with clipboard, stamps labeled 'auto-fix' and 'issue only', holding back a rushed robot

blocks.label: FINDING ROUTING
blocks.caption: the conservative default is a feature, not a bug.

bad.label: OPTIMISTIC AGENT
bad.html: "Looks like a bug. I will fix everything I think is weird."

good.label: CONSERVATIVE AGENT
good.html: "I only auto-fix when the criterion is explicit. If product or design must decide, I open an issue with evidence."

blocks.html: ← default safe, not heroic

blocks.title: Under the hood — reversible beats brave
blocks.html:
Fail-safe default means choosing the least destructive action when there is uncertainty. An unnecessary issue is easy to undo. A bad commit can steal an afternoon.

That is why the prompt repeats: when in doubt, issue; do not commit. Repetition here is not bad writing. It is a guardrail.

items.question: Why does decision-tree order matter?
items.answer: Because the first category that matches wins. Auto-fix categories must be more restrictive and come before conservative categories.

items.question: What is fall-through?
items.answer: When a finding does not clearly satisfy a criterion, it falls to the next one. If nothing is clear, it falls to the safe default.

blocks.prompt: In your automation, which findings could be auto-fixed and which should become issues?
blocks.hint: write observable criteria, not vibes: 500 error, failing test, truncated text, product uncertainty...

lessons.title: TDD is the fix's smoke detector

sticky.html: You do not ask the fire whether it is over. You look at the smoke detector. For a bug, the detector is a test that fails before and passes after.

blocks.html:
When the loop performs an auto-fix, it should first write a regression test that fails. Then it changes the code, runs checks, and reviews the diff. The test becomes an external sensor so the agent does not believe only its own story.

Without a test, the agent says "fixed it" with the same energy as a kid who shoved the mess under the bed.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: a red-beeping smoke detector next to a test labeled 'RED' turning into 'GREEN'

blocks.label: RED → GREEN → REVIEW
blocks.caption: the test does not prove everything, but it prevents a lot of theater.

blocks.title: fix branch — red/green guardrail

lines.text: git checkout -b fix/dogfood-login-email
pnpm test auth-email.spec.ts
✗ rejects invalid email — failing for the bug we found
# apply minimal code change
pnpm test auth-email.spec.ts && pnpm lint && pnpm build
✓ test green, lint green, build green
micro-review: diff minimal, no debug code, commit allowed

bad.label: RISKY
bad.html: "I changed the code, it looks right, I will commit to main."

good.label: QUARANTINE
good.html: "I created a local branch, wrote a regression test, passed checks, and left it for human review."

blocks.title: Under the hood — human-in-the-loop without the pretty speech
blocks.html:
An isolated branch is quarantine. The agent may produce work, but it does not decide alone that the work enters trunk. That boundary is a human checkpoint.

The prompt also includes micro-review: read its own diff, check for leftover debug code, confirm the change is minimal, and abandon if something smells wrong. That is self-critique; useful, but not a substitute for external review.

items.question: Why does the regression test come before the fix?
items.answer: To prove the test captures the bug. If it does not fail before, it may not be testing the real problem.

items.question: Why is "never push" a strong guardrail?
items.answer: Because push moves the problem outside the local area. A local branch is a lab; remote already starts becoming a team process.

items: the regression test fails before the fix
the test exercises the real bug, not a random failure
the diff is small and related only to the finding
lint and build pass after the fix
no console.log, debug branches, or commented-out experiments remain
the branch stays local until a human reviews it

blocks.prompt: What would be a good regression test for a bug you saw recently?
blocks.hint: phrase it as observable behavior: given X, when Y, then Z.

lessons.title: Bounded autonomy is a fenced yard

sticky.html: Picture a puppy in a yard. Inside the fence: run, sniff, chase leaves, be useful maybe. Outside the fence: traffic, lawsuits, and someone yelling your name.

blocks.html:
That is bounded autonomy. You do not remove agency; you shape the space where agency is allowed. The agent can explore, classify, open issues, write tests, and make small fixes — but only inside a policy that limits blast radius.

The fence is not there because the puppy is evil. The fence is there because the puppy has no concept of production.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: happy robot puppy inside a picket fence, with forbidden signs outside: trunk, push, no tests, infinite loop

blocks.label: THE AUTONOMY FENCE
blocks.caption: bounded autonomy is a permission boundary drawn in plain language.

bad.label: UNBOUNDED AGENT
bad.html:
“Keep working until the app is better. Make whatever changes you need.”

That is not delegation. That is dropping a chainsaw into a daycare.

good.label: BOUNDED AGENT
good.html: “For one scenario, run for 25 minutes, collect 5–7 findings, auto-fix only backend/tracking bugs with tests, max 3 fixes, max 50 LOC each, never push, open issues when unsure.”

blocks.label: THE FENCE IS THE FEATURE
blocks.html: Autonomy becomes useful when it is narrow. The trick is not “let the agent do anything.” The trick is “let the agent do a few clearly defined things repeatedly, safely, and audibly.”

items: one scenario per fire
hard timeout for each scenario run
maximum number of findings to document
maximum number of auto-fixes
maximum net LOC per fix
no commit to trunk, no push, branches stay local
issue-only default when classification is uncertain
kill criteria written before the loop starts

blocks.title: Under the hood — blast radius, reversibility, and permission design
blocks.html:
Blast radius means “how much damage can one mistake cause?” A loop with no caps can chew through a repo. A loop with one scenario, three small fixes, and no push can still be annoying, but it is reviewable.

Reversibility is the safety ranking. Opening an issue is easy to undo. Creating a local branch is reviewable. Pushing to trunk is not a toy. So the prompt biases toward reversible actions and makes destructive actions unavailable.

Permission design is not just tool permissions. It is also the shape of the prompt: what counts as auto-fixable, what must become an issue, what commands are allowed, and when the loop exits.

items.question: Why are “max 3 fixes” and “max 50 LOC” not arbitrary bureaucracy?
items.answer: They bound blast radius. Small outputs are easier to inspect, test, revert, and trust. Big autonomous rewrites are where review turns into archaeology.

items.question: What is the safest default when the agent is unsure?
items.answer: Open an issue with evidence. Do not commit code. The issue can be closed; a bad fix can hide in your tree and waste a day.

blocks.prompt: Take one recurring agent task you would like to automate. What fence would you draw around it?
blocks.hint: Name the allowed tools, forbidden actions, caps, review step, and kill criteria.

lessons.title: L4 autonomy is a self-driving car with an adult in the seat

sticky.html: The car drives on a fenced road, with a speed limit, a defined route, an emergency button, and someone watching. That is very different from "go anywhere and send me a postcard."

blocks.html:
The dogfood loop is fenced autonomy: hard caps, kill criteria, run log, scenario rotation, state file, and human review. It works alone for an interval, but it does not receive sovereignty.

An agent without limits is like a Roomba with a corporate card. It might clean, but you will want to check the bill.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: self-driving car on a fenced track, with cones labeled 'max 3 fixes', 'hard stop', 'no push' and an adult holding a red button

blocks.label: THE AUTONOMY FENCE
blocks.caption: each fence reduces the maximum damage of one bad decision.

blocks.title: dogfood-output/run-log.md — append only

lines.text: 2026-05-25 20:07 | /dogfood-login | findings=2 auto=1 ui=1 | fixes=1 | issues=1 | status=ok
2026-05-25 21:07 | /dogfood-checkout | findings=0 auto=0 ui=0 | fixes=0 | issues=0 | status=parked
2026-05-25 22:07 | /dogfood-onboarding | findings=1 auto=0 prod=1 | fixes=0 | issues=1 | status=ok
audit trail preserved; you can reconstruct what happened

bad.label: L5 FANTASY
bad.html: "Let the agent run until it solves everything and merges by itself."

good.label: HONEST L4
good.html: "Let the agent work inside limits, produce branches/issues/evidence, and stop when criteria hit. A human decides integration."

blocks.title: Under the hood — the three gaps you should say out loud
blocks.html:
First: no perfect external sensor for fixes; the agent still judges part of its own diff. Second: without an approved baseline for classification, the decision tree can be wrong. Third: context grows with every fire, so long sessions degrade and burn through the plan quickly. That is the practical meaning of the Twitter caveat about Max 20x burning fast: each hourly fire adds more transcript and tool output to the same session.

Saying these gaps out loud does not weaken the technique. It strengthens it. Honest capability disclosure is what separates engineering from a hypnotized demo.

items.question: What are kill criteria?
items.answer: Explicit conditions for stopping: hard stop reached, scenarios parked, runs with no findings, token/context limit, dirty tree, app unreachable.

items.question: Why does an append-only run log matter?
items.answer: Because auditing requires history. If the agent did something dumb at hour 7, you need to find hour 7, not hear "sorry, vibes."

blocks.prompt: What would make you comfortable reviewing an overnight agent loop: tests, logs, screenshots, branch policy, or something else?
blocks.hint: Design the review surface you would want before trusting the output.

outro.title: That is the AI + Dogfood Loop field guide.
outro.subtitle: Now go build a tiny loop, fence it in, and make it leave receipts.

stickies.html:
✎ remember:
the gist is not magic. it is a document that teaches an agent how to create its own operating procedure.

✎ next experiment:
write one scenario skill for a real flow, then run it once manually before scheduling anything.

✎ safety mantra:
state first. tests before fixes. issue when unsure. stop on weirdness.
